lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market custom and contract manufacturers within 326199 that mold, thermoform, fabricate, or assemble plastic components and products for external OEM, industrial, institutional, or private-label customers under recurring or repeat programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants; one-off consumer novelty, disposable houseware, seasonal recreational, resilient-flooring, and commodity retail-product models lacking recurring outsourced customer programs; pure distributors; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B program revenue based on part or product volumes, tooling and engineering charges, purchase orders, blanket orders, or supply agreements, with unit pricing periodically reset for resin, labor, freight, and competitive bids.
lens.deviation_from_default: The residual code spans unrelated products including cups, dinnerware, gloves, siding, floor coverings, inflatables, hardware, and trash containers. To keep one coherent screen, the lens retains repeat custom or contract B2B plastic-product supply and excludes consumer, seasonal, and project business models that have different acquisition, retention, and demand mechanics.

a.bridge: BLS measures four-digit NAICS 326100 occupational employment and wages, not task-level AI exposure for the narrowed 326199 contract-manufacturing lens. I map office, sales, engineering-support, quoting, planning, quality-documentation, inspection-support, and maintenance-triage tasks into a wage share; the residual code's diverse processes and broader BLS population create two-sided error.
a.rationale: Production occupations dominate broader plastics products, constraining direct AI substitution. Custom manufacturers also carry meaningful quoting, engineering coordination, scheduling, procurement, quality, compliance, and customer-service work, raising exposure above purely continuous commodity conversion.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from 15-20 eligible firms across molding, thermoforming, fabrication, and assembly, with current automation and time spent on quoting, engineering changes, scheduling, QA, inspection, and maintenance.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data do not isolate the residual code or narrowed lens.
The installed automation baseline and wage weighting by process are unknown.

rho.bridge: BLS describes automation and job change for metal and plastic machine workers economy-wide, not five-year implementation in eligible 326199 firms. I use the evidence of growing CNC, robotics, and digital-prototyping use as directional support; it may overstate smaller firms with varied jobs, legacy equipment, and limited integration talent.
rho.rationale: Quoting, scheduling, document, and analytics tools are implementable, but physical automation must handle varied molds, materials, finishes, inserts, secondary operations, and inspection requirements. High-mix plants face integration and change-management constraints.
rho.change_evidence: Firm-level automation roadmaps, MES and machine-vision penetration, integrator bids, AI project completion rates, payback evidence, and realized labor-hour changes by process family.
caveats: The occupation source mixes metal and plastics processes.
Implementation will vary sharply between high-volume molding and high-mix fabrication or assembly.

e.rationale: The narrowed lens excludes a material portion of this residual code's consumer, seasonal, retail, flooring, and captive operations. Among B2B custom manufacturers, further exclusions arise from customer concentration, weak tooling or equipment, environmental liabilities, nonrecurring projects, and economics outside the LMM band.
e.change_evidence: Product- and customer-level classification of the full establishment universe using D&B or Data Axle, websites and calls, followed by ownership, recurring-program share, captive status, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA verification.
caveats: Census examples demonstrate heterogeneity but do not quantify each submodel's firm share.
The frozen firm count is estimated from size classes and a margin bridge.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions of US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying transfers in custom plastics. The observed 22 percent employer-owner sell or transfer intention is discounted or widened for non-completion, internal transfers, closures, strategic absorption, and the age and ownership structure of eligible manufacturers.
s5.rationale: The broad employer-owner survey offers a useful anchor close to the base, but a qualifying control transfer requires a viable operating business and completed transaction. The large estimated universe should create deal opportunities even if the precise rate is uncertain.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transactions from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, plastics M&A advisers, and broker databases matched to a verified eligible-firm denominator, plus owner age and succession-plan status.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not completed transactions.
Transactions may be classified under customer end markets rather than 326199.

q.rationale: Custom tooling, engineering knowledge, qualification, quality history, and switching cost can protect benefits during programs. OEM sourcing teams still demand annual price reductions, resin transparency, competitive rebids, and productivity sharing, limiting long-run retention.
q.change_evidence: Twenty to thirty customer agreements and renewal histories showing contract length, productivity clauses, resin and freight pass-through, annual price-downs, rebid cadence, switching costs, and contribution margins around automation projects.
caveats: No public contract corpus measures retention.
Commodity products retain less than tightly qualified, low-volume, engineered parts.

d5.rationale: The narrowed portfolio serves diversified OEM and industrial end markets, so overall real demand should be broadly stable with potential reshoring upside. Foreign competition, lightweighting, recycled-content mandates, material substitution, customer insourcing, and end-market cycles produce meaningful downside and dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price shipment units by product and customer end market, customer program forecasts, backlog and award data, reshoring or insourcing announcements, and capacity additions by domestic custom molders.
caveats: No single forecast fits the code's many products.
The base is a portfolio judgment for the narrowed lens, not the full residual code.

o.rationale: Recurring programs still require an accountable physical converter to manage tooling, resin, process settings, testing, quality, traceability, and delivery. AI and software improve these functions but do not supply molded or fabricated products; insourcing and design or material substitution remain the displacement routes.
o.change_evidence: OEM make-versus-buy studies, insourcing announcements, program renewal and supplier-switch data, additive-manufacturing substitution evidence, and proof of fully software-supplied or unattended alternatives.
caveats: The physical-operator requirement is inferred rather than surveyed.
Product elimination and material substitution are primarily included in d5 to avoid double counting.

narrative.executive_view: The residual code is too diverse for one meaningful screen, so the analysis keeps repeat custom and contract B2B plastics manufacturing. That subset has useful AI opportunities in quoting, engineering coordination, scheduling, quality, inspection, maintenance, and administration, while physical conversion remains central.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can compress RFQ review, cost estimation, scheduling, work instructions, engineering-change handling, quality documentation, vision inspection, maintenance diagnosis, and customer support. High-mix molds, inserts, finishing, secondary operations, and line intervention constrain physical substitution.
narrative.value_capture: Tooling, qualification history, process know-how, and delivery performance create switching costs. OEM purchasing sophistication, annual productivity asks, resin transparency, and rebids nevertheless transfer a substantial share of visible gains to customers.
narrative.firm_availability: A large nominal firm universe is offset by the narrow eligible share and uncertain classification. Broad succession data support potential control flow, but product mix, ownership, recurring program quality, customer concentration, asset condition, and closed transactions need verification.
narrative.demand_durability: Diversified end markets stabilize the narrowed portfolio, while imports, substitution, insourcing, sustainability rules, and cycles can reduce specific programs. Physical products continue to require accountable manufacturing operators.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Heterogeneity is the dominant risk: firms sharing this code can have entirely different process, channel, demand, and margin structures. Other material risks are customer concentration, resin and freight volatility, obsolete presses and molds, environmental exposure, quality claims, and workforce scarcity.
narrative.principal_driver: A broad pool of repeat B2B manufacturers with engineering, planning, quality, and production-support workflows amenable to improvement.
narrative.principal_weakness: Residual-code heterogeneity makes eligibility, demand, and transfer estimates unusually uncertain.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 326100 Plastics Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Confirms coverage of NAICS 326100 employers and reports the industry occupation and wage mix used as the exposure proxy, including management, engineering, office, sales, production, maintenance, and material-moving work.

sources.title: Metal and Plastic Machine Workers, Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Describes machine setup, monitoring, adjustment, inspection, and documentation; reports continuing adoption of CNC, robotics, and digital prototyping; and notes foreign competition and potential reshoring.

sources.title: 2012 NAICS Definitions
sources.supports: Defines 326199 as plastics products outside the specifically named plastics categories and lists disparate examples including inflatables, air mattresses, bowls, hangers, cups, dinnerware, gloves, hardware, siding, trash containers, and resilient floor coverings, supporting the heterogeneous-lens narrowing.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners surveyed in fall 2024 planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, used as a cross-industry proxy rather than an observed plastics transaction rate.
